steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content: The sentence contains two complete thoughts separated by the blank: (1) “Her work challenges conventional notions of race, gender, history, [blank]” and (2) “she is credited with expanding the horizons of conceptual photographic art.” Each has its own subject and verb and can stand alone.

steps.title: Apply the rule for joining independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction
steps.content:
When a coordinating conjunction like “and” connects two independent clauses, standard English requires a comma before the conjunction—the pattern is: IC, and IC.

Choice A provides “memory, and,” which creates this exact structure.

Option B omits the required comma before “and.” Options C and D fail to provide any proper connector—C creates a comma splice (two independent clauses with only a comma), and D leaves the clauses fused with no punctuation at all.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
